accepted.title: Phase C→D window breaks every deal URL: Step B self-308 loops for backfilled rows, hard 404s for rows minted after the B backfill
accepted.claim: The resolver ladder deploys in Phase C while all stored slugs are still old-format (rewrite is Phase D) and legacy_slug = slug (plan.md:29). Old-format segments miss Step A (regex-verified), hit Step B, and 308 'to current canonical' = the same URL → infinite redirect loop site-wide for the whole C window. Rows inserted during C (old deals_set_slug trigger still active; mint triggers arrive only in D1) have NULL public_id and NULL legacy_slug → their live homepage-card URLs fall through all three steps → hard 404. The unconditional freeze (NEW.public_id := OLD.public_id) then pins NULL forever, and D1's slug rewrite composes slug_base||'-d'||NULL → NOT NULL violation aborts the flip (or strands the cohort). The Phase C checkpoint ('legacy URL → 308 → 200 one hop') is mathematically unsatisfiable pre-D, falsifying plan.md:8's independent-deployability claim.
accepted.evidence: Spec lines 79-88 (Step B has no segment===row.slug guard; no lookup on the live slug column); plan.md:8, 29, 42, 46-47; schema.sql:188-191 (old trigger); deals.repo.ts:19 and ingest-awin.cjs:136 (writers keep composing old-format slugs through C); regex non-match of old-format slugs verified by node; spec line 51 (unconditional freeze); schema.sql:197 (slug NOT NULL).
accepted.fix: Spec §4 Step B (lines 85-88): rewrite to 'Step B — exact .eq(slug, raw) on the live slug column → 200 self-canonical (covers the pre-flip window); then exact .eq(legacy_slug, raw), then lower() variant → if row.slug === raw render 200, else 308 to current canonical.' Plan Phase B: move the mint triggers to B — BEFORE INSERT sets public_id := coalesce(NEW.public_id, substr(md5(NEW.product_id),1,12)) AND legacy_slug := coalesce(NEW.legacy_slug, NEW.slug); BEFORE UPDATE freezes NULL-safely (NEW.public_id := COALESCE(OLD.public_id, substr(md5(NEW.product_id),1,12))). Plan D1: add a pre-rewrite delta backfill (UPDATE deals SET public_id=…, legacy_slug=coalesce(legacy_slug,slug) WHERE public_id IS NULL OR legacy_slug IS NULL) plus the gate assertion count(*)=0 before the one-time slug UPDATE. Plan C checkpoint (line 42): change to 'old-format URL sampled from prod DB → 200 with self-canonical (no redirect) pre-flip'. Todo C2 acceptance: add the equality guard + phase-aware smoke assertions. Plan: add 'rollback floor' note — after D1, full URL compatibility requires the C build or newer (pre-C builds 404 previously-published old-format URLs, though on-site nav still works); hold C in prod ≥1 week before D.

accepted.title: Expiry re-activation trigger has no discriminator — both naive implementations of §5 are broken, and the unguarded historical-lows RPC compounds it
accepted.claim: Spec §5 says the cron sets status='expired' and 'any later upsert re-activates (trigger clears status/expired_at)', but at trigger level the cron's UPDATE, the update_historical_lows_batch RPC's UPDATE (runs after every refresh, no value-change guard), and a writer's ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE are indistinguishable. If the trigger clears status on any UPDATE, the cron's own write is reverted (nothing ever expires); if it waits for writers to send status, it never fires (neither toRow() nor the ingest payload contains a status key, and PostgREST merge-duplicates touches only payload columns). Downgraded from blocker: D3's staging verification ('age a row, cron marks it, upsert re-activates') would catch either failure mode before prod, and the fix is a one-predicate spec addition.
accepted.evidence: deals.repo.ts:18-31,60 (no status key, blind upsert); ingest-awin.cjs:139-163, 226-236 (no status key; Prefer: resolution=merge-duplicates); schema.sql:144-155 (RPC SET has no change guard); route.ts:64 (RPC runs after every refresh). ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE fires BEFORE UPDATE row triggers — the mechanism is viable, only the discriminator is unspecified.
accepted.fix: Spec §5 line 99: replace 'Any later upsert re-activates (trigger clears status/expired_at)' with the exact predicate: in the BEFORE UPDATE trigger, IF NEW.last_updated IS DISTINCT FROM OLD.last_updated THEN NEW.status := 'active'; NEW.expired_at := NULL; END IF — writers always send a fresh last_updated; the expiry cron and update_historical_lows_batch never touch it. Add: 'writers MUST NOT send status/expired_at.' Todo D3 verify: add 'cron-expire a row → run updateHistoricalLows → assert still expired; re-upsert → assert active.'

accepted.title: revalidate = 3600 on the deal route is dead config: the shared [locale] layout calls cookies(), forcing every deal page dynamic
accepted.claim: Spec §4's cache-bounding rationale ('so Netlify can't pin a stale 308') assumes the deal route is ISR-cacheable. It is not: layout.tsx line 46 awaits cookies() for LocationProvider, and in Next 14 a dynamic function anywhere in the route tree opts the whole route into dynamic rendering, making route-level revalidate meaningless. Consequence fork the spec must resolve: (a) fully-dynamic deal pages — every crawl of N deals × 13 locales is a function invocation + Supabase query, unbudgeted; or (b) a layout/location refactor to earn ISR, which the spec neither scopes nor mentions.
accepted.evidence: src/app/[locale]/layout.tsx:46 (await cookies()); the deal page renders under this layout; the codebase's own convention confirms the constraint (category page.tsx:11 export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' with cookie-country comment). Next 14 documented behavior: dynamic functions opt the route into request-time rendering; revalidate applies to statically rendered routes only.
accepted.fix: Spec §4 line 93: pick one explicitly. Option (a): delete the revalidate rationale, add a crawl-load budget line (Supabase QPS + Netlify invocation cost for a full-sitemap crawl) to Phase E acceptance. Option (b): add a C-phase task to move the cookies() read out of the shared [locale] layout (route group so /deal/* doesn't inherit it, or client-side location hydration) and only then keep revalidate=3600. Update todo C2 acceptance accordingly.

accepted.title: buildDealPath(locale, slug) fed to next-intl Link double-prefixes the locale — /en/en/deal/… 404 on every card click
accepted.claim: Spec §4 mandates both that all 6 construction sites route through ONE helper returning `/${locale}/deal/${slug}` (code-style snippet, lines 156-158) AND that DealCard switches to the next-intl Link. These are mutually exclusive: createNavigation's Link under localePrefix:'always' prepends the locale itself and expects locale-less hrefs. One signature cannot serve both the absolute-URL sites (canonical/hreflang/JSON-LD/sitemap) and the Link.
accepted.evidence: src/i18n/routing.ts:11,14 (localePrefix:'always', createNavigation); repo's own convention proves the split: CategoryMenu passes locale-less paths to next-intl Link while DealCard.tsx:28-29 manually prefixes with plain next/link — exactly what C4 changes. Route shape /[locale]/deal/[slug] cannot match /en/en/deal/x.
accepted.fix: Spec §4 line 94 + code-style snippet: split into `dealHref(slug)` → `/deal/${slug}` (next-intl Link consumers) and `absoluteDealUrl(locale, slug)` → `${BASE_URL}/${locale}/deal/${slug}` (metadata canonical/hreflang, JSON-LD, sitemap), both wrapping one composition point. State that the resolver's 308 uses next/navigation's permanentRedirect with the fully-prefixed path (not createNavigation's redirect). Todo C4 acceptance: add a unit test that the DealCard href contains exactly one locale segment.

accepted.title: Next 14 generateSitemaps() produces no sitemap index — 'under an index' and E2's 'submit sitemap index' reference an artifact nothing creates, and /sitemap.xml goes 404
accepted.claim: generateSitemaps emits only shard files at /sitemap/[id].xml; no <sitemapindex> is generated, and the root /sitemap.xml stops existing once shards are exported. So robots.ts's single derived Sitemap: line points at a 404 (violating success criterion 3 — robots.txt has advertised /sitemap.xml since launch), E2 has nothing to submit to GSC, shard-count growth has no discovery path, and C5's verify step ('local build emits sitemap index + shards') can never pass — doubly so since Next 14 dev serves shards at /sitemap.xml/[id], not the prod path.
accepted.evidence: Next 14 generate-sitemaps docs describe only /…/sitemap/[id].xml output; no index API exists (vercel/next.js #61025, #77304). Spec line 105; plan.md:56; todo.md C5/C6, E2; public/robots.txt Sitemap line.
accepted.fix: Spec §6 + Project structure: add an explicit deliverable, e.g. src/app/sitemap-index.xml/route.ts, emitting <sitemapindex> entries for ceil(activeCount/shardSize) shards from a live count query; robots.ts Sitemap: points at it; E2 submits that URL. Rewrite todo C5's verify step to check prod-shape URLs on `next start` (not dev), noting the dev/prod path difference.

accepted.title: sitemap.ts is build-time frozen: no revalidate/dynamic config specified, so expiry-pruning, freshness lastmod, and new-deal entry all stop between deploys
accepted.claim: Next 14 metadata routes are cached/prerendered at build unless they use a dynamic API or route-segment config; the spec pins revalidate only on the deal route (line 93) and specifies nothing for sitemap.ts/robots.ts. With nightly ingest and pg_cron expiry happening without deploys, expired deals stay listed, new deals never enter, lastmod never moves — success criterion 4 is unfalsifiable-false. Shard COUNT from generateSitemaps is likewise fixed at build. The live production sitemap is consistent with this: it holds only the 143 static entries (spec line 13) even though src/app/sitemap.ts:42-57 already emits deals whenever the query succeeds, with the failure swallowed at :55-57.
accepted.evidence: Next 14 sitemap file-convention docs ('cached by default unless…'); spec §6 and file list contain no cache requirement for sitemap.ts; src/app/sitemap.ts:30-59 (no dynamic config; catch swallows errors); spec's own line 13 prod probe vs the code.
accepted.fix: Spec §6: specify `export const revalidate = 3600` (or force-dynamic) on sitemap.ts and the index route; define shard-count growth handling (index route computes count per request; over-allocate shards or a redeploy-before-N×shardSize rule); stop swallowing the getAllDealSlugs error — an empty deal sitemap must fail loudly. Phase E1: add a deploy-preview freshness check (post-deploy DB insert appears in a shard after TTL; check Cache-Status headers).

accepted.title: Sitemap shard query: PostgREST max-rows (default 1000) silently truncates every shard, the new partial index doesn't serve the query, and mutable NULL-heavy ordering makes shard membership unstable
accepted.claim: Three compounding defects. (1) Spec line 193's 'sharding fixes it regardless' is false: max-rows caps every response, so a 10k .range() window returns 1000 rows and rows 1000-9999 of each window appear in NO shard — silent ~90% loss, worse than today's visible 5000 cap. (2) The only new index is (last_updated DESC) WHERE status='active' (line 98) but the query orders by content_changed_at (line 106) — full scan+sort per shard render. (3) Phase B's backfill omits content_changed_at entirely, so the whole pre-migration catalog is NULL (which sorts NULLS FIRST under DESC), and a writer-bumped mutable sort key + OFFSET pagination reshuffles rows across shard boundaries between Google's separate fetches — duplicated and skipped URLs as steady state, breaking criterion 4.
accepted.evidence: Spec lines 98 vs 105-107 vs 193 (verbatim contradictions); plan.md:29 (backfill list has no content_changed_at); Supabase max-rows default 1000 / PostgREST db-max-rows semantics; DESC ⇒ NULLS FIRST is PG default; current code already shows the cap class (deals.repo.ts:189 .limit(5000)).
accepted.fix: Plan Phase B: add `content_changed_at := last_updated` to the backfill list. Spec §6: shard on an immutable key — keyset-paginate ORDER BY public_id with fixed ranges (keep lastmod = content_changed_at as a field, not the sort; if freshness bias is wanted, apply it within shard 0 only); add partial index matching whatever the query actually orders by, WHERE status='active'; paginate each shard fetch internally in ≤1000-row .range() pages (or raise max-rows as a documented deploy prerequisite). Delete the 'sharding fixes it regardless' sentence (the A2 probe stays). Todo C8: add a count-parity assertion — |sitemap URLs| equals the DB active count within tolerance, and expired rows are absent.

accepted.title: Sitemap entry shape is wrong twice: Google's hreflang-sitemap method needs a <url> per locale variant, and 10k-entry × 14-alternate shards (~23 MB) exceed Netlify's function response limits
accepted.claim: Spec line 108 claims '<loc> = default-locale variant … is the documented Google pattern' — it is the opposite: Google's localized-versions doc requires a separate <url> element for EVERY variant, each carrying the full alternate set. As specced, the 12 non-default self-canonical URLs per deal appear in no sitemap. Separately, the shard size is set against the protocol limit (50k/50MB) not the host: ~2.3 KB/entry with 14 xhtml:link alternates × 10k ≈ 23 MB per per-request-generated response, past Netlify's 6 MB synchronous cap (and ~20 MB streaming). Downgraded from the attacker's 'blocker': on-page hreflang (already emitted by the deal page) preserves variant discovery/canonicalization, so this degrades rather than breaks indexing.
accepted.evidence: Google localized-versions doc: 'Create a separate <url> element for each URL as you would with any other sitemap' (its example emits one <url> per variant); current code already encodes the wrong pattern (src/app/sitemap.ts:22 comment); byte math from the current serializer shape (sitemap.ts:9-27, 14-language alternates) — verified ≈2.3 KB/entry; Netlify 6 MB sync payload limit; netlify.toml confirms functions serve these routes.
accepted.fix: Spec §6 line 108: delete 'this is the documented Google pattern'. Choose ONE method and state it: (a) emit 13 <url> entries per deal each with the identical alternates cluster — then entries = active_deals × 13 and shard size must be host-derived (~2-2.5k entries/shard ≈ 5-6 MB); or (b) drop sitemap-level alternates entirely, list all 13 plain locale URLs, and rely on the on-page hreflang the deal page already emits (Google: don't mix methods). Write the chosen byte math into the spec; update success criterion 4 and C5 acceptance.

accepted.title: content_changed_at 'bumped by writers' is impossible with blind full-row upserts, and the bump criterion references an 'availability' column that does not exist
accepted.claim: Both writers are blind upserters with no read of the existing row (deals.repo.ts:60; ingest-awin.cjs streamed REST batches), so a writer cannot know whether price/name changed: it either always sends now() — inflating lastmod on every refresh, the exact failure the design exists to avoid — or SELECT-before-upsert, doubling round trips and racing the concurrency-6 refresh fan-out. Grep-verified: no availability column exists in schema.sql or NormalizedDeal.
accepted.evidence: deals.repo.ts:60; ingest-awin.cjs:226-236; route.ts:46-62; grep 'availability' over supabase/schema.sql + src/lib/providers/types.ts → zero hits; spec line 107 contradicts the spec's own trigger-authoritative deploy-order argument at line 70.
accepted.fix: Spec §6 line 107 + plan D2 + todo D2: move the bump into the authoritative trigger — BEFORE INSERT: NEW.content_changed_at := now(); BEFORE UPDATE: IF ROW(NEW.product_name, NEW.brand, NEW.sale_price, NEW.original_price, NEW.discount_percent) IS DISTINCT FROM ROW(OLD.…) THEN now() ELSE OLD.content_changed_at, force-overwriting any writer-supplied value. Delete 'add content_changed_at bump logic to both writers' from plan D2/todo D2. Drop 'availability' from the criterion or define the column first.

accepted.title: 48h expiry is justified against a fictional 15-min cadence — the deployed scheduler is daily with a top-100-per-cell persistence cap, so live deals mass-expire, rank-100 rows flap, and two missed crons expire the catalog
accepted.claim: Spec lines 99/194 justify 48h via '15-min refresh + nightly ingest', but the only deployed scheduler is netlify/functions/refresh-deals.mts at '0 4 * * *' (daily; the 15-min text is an aspirational comment), and each run persists at most 100 deals per country×category (route.ts:53, registry slice). Consequences: any deal below rank ~100 in its cell is never re-touched → expired at 48h while still live (JSON-LD flips to OutOfStock on purchasable offers — a trust problem on the exact evidence pages the spec wants cited); rank-≈100 rows flap active↔expired, churning sitemap membership; steady-state active set caps near 16×10×100 + the AWIN feed; two missed daily runs (no retry; a rotated CRON_SECRET 401s silently) mass-expire everything. No cron monitoring/alerting exists anywhere in spec/plan/todo.
accepted.evidence: netlify/functions/refresh-deals.mts:33 (schedule '0 4 * * *'); route.ts:53 (limit: 100); registry.ts:121 (slice(0, limit)); .github/workflows/ingest-awin.yml (nightly, cron '0 3 * * *'); GitHub Actions schedule delay/drop caveat is documented.
accepted.fix: Spec §5 line 99 + open question 3: re-derive the window from the real cadence — key expiry to availability signals, not write recency: feed sources expire by set-difference against the latest SUCCESSFUL ingest run (skip expiry entirely if the last run failed); API sources either get a full-inventory sweep on a slower cadence or are exempted; if wall-clock stays, ≥72h minimum. Add a tripwire: the cron refuses to expire >N% of active rows in one run. Add ingest/refresh failure alerting as an explicit deliverable. Document the steady-state active-set cap and rank-flapping, or raise the per-cell refresh limit.

accepted.title: No read path except the sitemap filters status='active' — listings and brand menus rot as the never-deleted table trends majority-expired
accepted.claim: queryDeals (homepage/category grids, default sort discount_percent DESC), distinct_brands, and getRecentlyUpdatedDeals are untouched by the spec (file list lines 130-147) and filter nothing today. With rows never deleted and 48h expiry, high-discount expired deals occupy page 1 of every listing forever with enabled CTAs, and brand filters offer brands with zero live deals.
accepted.evidence: deals.repo.ts:82-113 (no status filter, discount-DESC default), :115-127; schema.sql:43-52 (distinct_brands RPC has no status predicate); spec §5 defines read-path behavior only for the deal page and sitemap.
accepted.fix: Spec §5: add a read-path matrix — queryDeals, distinct_brands, and search gain status='active' predicates; deal page and price-alert notify may return expired rows; state it explicitly. Make the hot-path indexes (deals_country_discount_idx etc.) partial WHERE status='active' so page-1 scans stay cheap as the expired fraction grows. Add the repo/RPC changes to the C2/C5 file list.

accepted.title: Collision odds understated ~178×, and the 16-hex runbook mints a URL the spec's own parser can never route; a collision also becomes a deterministic nightly ingest outage
accepted.claim: Verified by computation: 48-bit birthday probability is 1.78e-5 at 100k rows and 1.77e-3 at 1M (spec's ~10⁻⁷ corresponds to ~7,500 rows); with rows never deleted, collision is a when, not if. The runbook 'lengthen to 16 hex' produces slug '…-d{16hex}' which fails /^(.*)-d([0-9a-f]{12})$/ (regex-verified), misses Step B (no matching legacy_slug), and 404s forever — lengthening the OTHER row instead breaks its already-published d{12} URL. Meanwhile a unique violation aborts the 500-row batch and the same feed row re-aborts every nightly run until manual action, with no alerting. Bonus defect found in adjudication: the spec's own §1 example slug (-d3f8a1c92b4e) contains only 11 hex chars and fails its own regex.
accepted.evidence: Computed: P=1.776e-5 @1e5, 1.775e-3 @1e6, 4.34e-2 @5e6; 9.99e-8 @7500. node regex test: 'base-d'+16hex → no match; 'd3f8a1c92b4e' id length = 11. ingest-awin.cjs:236 (throw aborts), spec lines 53, 79, 153, 182.
accepted.fix: Spec §2 line 53: correct the probability figures (1.8×10⁻⁵ @100k; 1.8×10⁻³ @1M) and delete the 16-hex runbook. Replace with in-trigger self-healing: on public_id unique conflict at INSERT, deterministically re-derive from salted input (substr(md5(product_id || ':1'),1,12), incrementing the salt until unique) so length stays 12, the parser is untouched, and ingest never aborts — documenting that a collided row loses offline derivability. Fix the §1 example to a 12-hex id. Add ingest-failure alerting to the deliverables (also required by the expiry finding).

accepted.title: Brand-prefix predicate treats brand as an ILIKE pattern and compares in raw text space — wildcard brands corrupt suppression, accented brands mint permanently duplicated slugs, and it cannot be mirrored in TS
accepted.claim: product_name NOT ILIKE brand || '%' (spec lines 61-62) has two defects: unescaped % / _ in free-text feed brands act as wildcards ('100% Pure' wrongly matches '1000W Pure Sine Inverter' → brand suppressed), and the test runs pre-normalization while output is unaccent/lowered — brand "L'Oréal" vs feed name "L'Oreal Paris …" fails the prefix test (é≠e) → permanent slug 'l-oreal-l-oreal-paris-…'. Same class for Müller/Muller across the DACH/Nordic feeds. ILIKE wildcard semantics also can't be byte-mirrored in the TS parity fixtures the spec mandates (line 169). Slugs are permanent once minted, so this must land before Phase B/D.
accepted.evidence: PostgreSQL LIKE/ILIKE right operand is a pattern (% and _ wildcards, \ escape); ingest-awin.cjs:150 (brand_name is untrimmed free text); spec lines 61-63 vs 169.
accepted.fix: Spec §3: replace the predicate with a wildcard-free comparison in slug space: norm_b := pipeline(brand); norm_n := pipeline(product_name); prepend brand iff norm_n <> norm_b AND left(norm_n, char_length(norm_b) + 1) <> norm_b || '-'. Add '100% Pure' and L'Oréal-class cases to the parity fixtures (spec Testing strategy bullet 1).

accepted.title: Country last-write-wins identity flap leaks into this spec's own deliverables: permanent URLs minted on known-wrong identity, content_changed_at inflated every cycle, JSON-LD currency oscillating under a stable canonical
accepted.claim: One row per product_id (upsert onConflict product_id) with country/currency/price overwritten by whichever of the 16-market fan-out tasks wrote last; kelkoo productIds carry no country (verified: `kelkoo:${o.id}`, country from the query), so cross-market id overlap flaps rows on every refresh. Inside this spec: (a) flapping rows get real price/currency changes each cycle → the trigger-side content_changed_at bump fires every run → the inflated-lastmod behavior the spec says Google punishes, breaking criterion 4; (b) JSON-LD priceCurrency oscillates EUR↔GBP↔PLN between crawls; (c) the line-202 escape hatch is only 1/16 true — a UNIQUE frozen public_id can follow exactly one successor row in a future country-split, so 15 markets mint new URLs anyway. Deferring this (line 202) while minting 'permanent' URLs is a design decision, not an implementation detail. Confidence: mechanism confirmed in code; cross-country id overlap for Kelkoo is likely but unproven — add a pre-flight query.
accepted.evidence: deals.repo.ts:60; route.ts:32-53 (16×10 fan-out); src/lib/providers/kelkoo.ts:85 (productId: `kelkoo:${o.id}`, country: query.country); ingest-awin.cjs:36 (COUNTRY CLI flag, currently single-country nightly so AWIN flap is latent); page.tsx:23 (resolver drops country); spec lines 107, 185, 202.
accepted.fix: Spec Assumptions + Out-of-scope line 202: escalate to a Phase A reviewer decision — either (a) fold country into product_id at the provider boundary NOW (kelkoo:DE:123; mock-data.ts already does provider:COUNTRY-…), accepting N URLs per product, or (b) explicitly commit to one-row-per-product, exempt country/currency-driven price flips from the content_changed_at bump, and document that the future country-split hands the frozen public_id to exactly one successor. Add an A2 pre-flight query: count product_ids written by >1 country in the last 7 days.

accepted.title: Mutable slug_base + permanently-cached 308s turns feed-title oscillation into client-side redirect loops and lets one brand backfill 308-churn the whole corpus
accepted.claim: slug_base recomputes on any name/brand change (spec line 69) and drift heals via 308, which browsers cache indefinitely (RFC 7538). A title flip A→B→A leaves a user's browser with cached S1→S2 while the server now serves S2→S1 → ERR_TOO_MANY_REDIRECTS client-side with no server-side fix until cache eviction. A brand backfill (NULL→value on thousands of rows) rewrites every affected slug_base in one trigger pass → corpus-wide recanonicalization. The spec records no rationale for recompute-on-change over freeze-after-mint even though Step A already makes stale bases cosmetically harmless — the spec explicitly accepts cosmetic staleness for truncation/case variants (line 82).
accepted.evidence: Spec lines 69, 82, 94; RFC 7538 §3 (308 cacheable like 301); feed-title mutability in kelkoo.ts (productName = o.title each refresh) and ingest-awin.cjs:134; the Netlify adapter additionally has an open redirect-response caching bug class (opennextjs-netlify #3460) injecting max-age=86400.
accepted.fix: Spec §3 line 69: put the choice to the reviewer as a Phase A decision with a stated default of FREEZE — slug_base computed at first mint, recomputed only by explicit admin action (cost: cosmetic staleness the spec already accepts). If recompute is kept: serve drift-healing as 307 and reserve 308 for the legacy layer, add oscillation hysteresis, and a churn-budget alert (>X% slug rewrites/day).

accepted.title: Discovery starvation: the only crawl path to deal pages is a cookie-gated, force-dynamic, unpaginated 48-item hub — virtually the whole inventory is sitemap-only
accepted.claim: Category hubs derive country from a geo cookie Googlebot never sends and render at most 48 deals with no page parameter, so at most 48 × |categories| deals in the crawler-default country have ANY internal link; everything else relies purely on sitemap discovery, which at this scale commonly yields 'Discovered — currently not crawled'. The spec owns the sitemap and C7 category metadata but not a single crawl path, and puts canonical/hreflang on pages whose content varies by cookie rather than URL — a locale-adaptive-content pattern Google explicitly warns about.
accepted.evidence: src/app/[locale]/category/[slug]/page.tsx:11 (force-dynamic), :31-33 (cookie country → DEFAULT_COUNTRY), :46 (limit: 48, no page searchParam); active deal pages link only home (page.tsx:110) — no related-deal links; spec line 110 scope.
accepted.fix: Spec §6 line 110 / todo C7: add minimum crawl-path work — crawlable category pagination (?page=N with self-canonical per page), a stable crawler-default country per locale (Googlebot is cookieless), and related-deal links on LIVE deal pages (the spec currently gives same-category links only to the expired state). Alternatively scope success criteria down: state that discovery is sitemap-only and 'rank every deal URL' is not a claim this spec makes.

accepted.title: Expired-forever-200 pages become orphaned soft-404 bait at scale, and the '410 valve' has no representable state under CHECK (status IN ('active','expired'))
accepted.claim: Google classifies 200 pages whose content signals unavailability as soft 404s and deindexes them — so the stated AEO payoff (citable evidence pages) self-defeats, while at 100k+ rows the pages are sitemap-less, internally-unlinked orphans that still consume crawl budget. The Slickdeals precedent doesn't transfer (their expired pages carry heavy UGC; these are thin: banner + disabled CTA, with the 90-day-low proof line explicitly dropped). And the escape hatch isn't one: the CHECK constraint admits no 'gone' state, so enabling the 410 valve later requires an ALTER + resolver changes — it's an unbuilt feature, not a valve.
accepted.evidence: Spec §5 lines 98 (CHECK two states), 100-101; plan.md accepted trade-offs; Google http-network-errors doc (content suggesting an error → soft 404); combined with the expiry-cadence finding, steady state is a corpus overwhelmingly of such pages.
accepted.fix: Spec §5: (1) add 'gone' to the CHECK now and define resolver behavior (Step A hit with status='gone' → 410); (2) make the valve a scheduled policy default — 410 for rows expired >12 months with zero impressions — and restate the guarantee as 'no URL ever 404s unexpectedly; retirement is 410 by policy'; (3) make expired pages non-thin: render the retained price-history table, not just a banner; (4) optionally give recently-expired rows a bounded dedicated shard (lastmod=expired_at, ≤12 months) plus internal links from live same-category pages.

accepted.title: Empty-env dev breaks: mock providers never set slug, no mock branch is specced for getDealByPublicId, and C4's md5-in-DealCard is unimplementable in a 'use client' component
accepted.claim: NormalizedDeal.slug is optional and no provider assigns it (grep-verified), so the mock branches of getDealBySlug/getAllDealSlugs can never produce or resolve a -d{hex12} segment → after C4, every deal page 404s in empty-env dev, breaking the repo's documented invariant (deals.repo.ts:4-6). C4 also puts the md5-12 fallback inside DealCard, which is 'use client': Node crypto is unavailable, SubtleCrypto has no MD5, and the spec forbids new deps. Criterion 7 ('same physical deal, dev vs prod, byte-identical') is additionally vacuous — mock productIds (provider:COUNTRY-category-i) never exist in prod.
accepted.evidence: types.ts:46-47; grep of src/lib/providers → no slug assignment; deals.repo.ts:143, 183; DealCard.tsx:1 ('use client'); mock-data.ts productId shape (verified); todo C2/C4/C5 acceptance silent on mock resolution.
accepted.fix: Todo C2: extend acceptance — getDealByPublicId's mock branch matches on md5-12(productId), computed server-side. Todo C4: DealCard never computes md5; the repo/registry mock read path stamps deal.slug (Node context) via the shared helper. Todo C5: mock getAllDealSlugs composes slugify(name)+'-d'+md5_12(productId). Spec criterion 7: rewrite as 'mock URLs match the prod FORMAT regex'. Add one vitest case: mock DealCard href → mock resolver → 200.

accepted.title: Cache/purge semantics are misdescribed: deploys auto-purge (ODB is v4 vocabulary), the real exposure is the DB-only Phase D flip, and the one-hop success criteria need a staleness qualifier
accepted.claim: The spec's 'ODB cache behavior is unverified — schedule a cache purge' and plan C/E1 purge steps name no mechanism; verified reality is that Netlify atomic deploys invalidate all CDN objects and the Next route cache is deploy-scoped blobs — app deploys purge automatically. The unhandled case is the inverse: Phase D's one-time slug UPDATE is a DB write not synchronized with a deploy, so cached old-canonical 200s and any cached 308s survive up to TTL(+SWR), transiently violating the unconditional 'always ONE hop' criteria 2-3, and the smoke suite will flake right after staged renames. Mid-window purges need the Purge API (purgeCache()), not the UI 'Clear cache and retry deploy' (build cache only).
accepted.evidence: Netlify caching-overview (atomic-deploy invalidation of all cached objects; durable cache is function-scoped); opennextjs-netlify uses getDeployStore (deploy-scoped); spec lines 83, 93, 171, 183-184; plan.md:42, 54; adapter redirect-caching bug #3460 argues for header assertions.
accepted.fix: Spec Testing/Post-deploy + plan C-risk/E1: replace the ODB sentence with 'app deploys auto-purge (atomic invalidation + deploy-scoped route-cache blobs); for DB-only slug changes between deploys, call purgeCache() (site- or tag-scoped)'. Sequence Phase D as UPDATE → immediate app deploy (or API purge). Qualify criteria 2/3 and the smoke one-hop assertions with 'within one cache window of the rename' (or warm-up request first). E1 curl matrix: assert cache-control/Cache-Status on 308 responses, not just status codes.

accepted.title: The spec's own verification gates are toothless: the criterion-6 grep misses 5 of 8 composition sites (including the two D2 exists to clean up), and 'green in CI' references a CI that doesn't exist
accepted.claim: Run verbatim, grep -rn "slugify(d.productName)" matches only deals.repo.ts:19/143/183 — it cannot match ingest-awin.cjs:136, DealCard.tsx:28, fromRow's fallback at deals.repo.ts:36 (absent from D2's deletion scope, contradicting 'the quadruplication dies'), or even the registry.ts:108 dedupe key it exempts. And .github/workflows contains only three cron jobs (db-migrate, ingest-awin, purge-alerts); netlify.toml builds with bare `pnpm build` — nothing runs vitest/typecheck/smoke anywhere, and smoke-spine needs a live BASE_URL, so 'green in CI' implies an unplanned deploy-preview workflow.
accepted.evidence: Both greps executed during adjudication: verbatim pattern → 3 hits, broad pattern slugify\([^)]*[Nn]ame\) → 8 hits across 5 files; ls .github/workflows → 3 cron yml files; netlify.toml verified.
accepted.fix: Spec criterion 6: gate on grep -rnE "slugify\([^)]*[Nn]ame\)" src scripts with an explicit allowlist (registry.ts dedupe key + the mock-parity helper). Todo D2: add fromRow (deals.repo.ts:36) and DealCard.tsx:28 to the deletion scope. Add a task: .github/workflows/ci.yml running typecheck + vitest + check-i18n on PR, plus a deploy-preview-triggered smoke-spine job — or reword criterion 6 to 'green against deploy preview, run manually' and fix D3's phantom 'Files: CI config' reference.

accepted.title: TS↔SQL slug parity is unachievable for ß/ø/æ/đ: NFD slugify deletes what unaccent transliterates
accepted.claim: The TS mirror mandated by the testing strategy (line 169) would reuse slugify()'s NFD + combining-mark strip, but ß/ø/æ/đ have no NFD decomposition and are deleted outright (computation-verified: 'Weißwein' → 'wei-wein', 'Søstrene' → 's-strene') while Postgres unaccent transliterates them (ß→ss, ø→o, æ→ae). Five of the 13 locales are saturated with these characters, so the parity fixtures are either cherry-picked ASCII or the mirror needs a hand-maintained transliteration table — a new permanent drift surface in a spec whose purpose is killing slug-formula duplication.
accepted.evidence: node verification of src/lib/utils/slug.ts:8-9 pipeline on ß/ø/æ/đ; PostgreSQL unaccent default rules transliterate ligatures/ß.
accepted.fix: Spec Testing strategy: make SQL the only authority — a script runs deal_slug_base() against the fixture inputs and emits a JSON snapshot; TS tests compare against the snapshot instead of maintaining a live TS mirror of the pipeline. If a TS mirror must exist for the mock path, scope the parity claim to [a-z0-9] input and list ß/ø/æ/đ as known-divergent.

accepted.title: deal_slug_base() declared IMMUTABLE while calling STABLE unaccent(), with unqualified schema resolution on Supabase
accepted.claim: unaccent is upstream-declared STABLE (rules files change across PG majors; the one-arg form resolves its dictionary via search_path), so the IMMUTABLE label is false, unnecessary for trigger-only use, and a latent index-corruption footgun if anyone later adds an expression index or GENERATED column. On Supabase, extensions install into the extensions schema, so unqualified unaccent(...) depends on the caller's search_path (PostgREST vs pg_cron vs SQL editor differ) and trips the platform linter.
accepted.evidence: contrib/unaccent declares both signatures STABLE; CREATE FUNCTION volatility is unvalidated by Postgres; spec line 58; Supabase extensions-schema convention + linter 0011.
accepted.fix: Spec §3: declare deal_slug_base() STABLE and schema-qualify the call — extensions.unaccent('extensions.unaccent'::regdictionary, $1) or ALTER FUNCTION … SET search_path = public, extensions; migration uses CREATE EXTENSION unaccent WITH SCHEMA extensions. Note: if an expression index is ever wanted, use a separate deliberately-IMMUTABLE two-arg wrapper with a REINDEX-on-PG-upgrade note.

accepted.title: Middleware geo reads x-nf-country, which the Netlify Next runtime likely never sends — silent DE default for all visitors (pre-existing; verify, don't assume)
accepted.claim: src/middleware.ts:13-18 derives the dr_location cookie from x-nf-country (then Vercel/CF headers) with a silent 'DE' fallback; the Netlify runtime v5 delivers geo via the structured context.geo → NextRequest.geo object, and the attacker found no x-nf-country injection in the adapter source. If correct, every first-visit user in all 16 markets gets a 30-day DE cookie with zero error signal — which also skews what content the cookie-gated hubs serve. Downgraded from major: pre-existing code outside this spec's file list, and the header's absence is asserted from adapter-source search, not observed behavior.
accepted.evidence: src/middleware.ts:12-28 (verified: header chain + 'DE' fallback + 30-day cookie); Netlify docs expose geo via context.geo; claim of zero x-nf-country hits in opennextjs-netlify is the attacker's, unverified here.
accepted.fix: Add to Phase E (or C-adjacent cleanup): middleware prefers req.geo?.country before the header fallbacks (Next 14 NextRequest supports it and it is what the Netlify runtime populates), keeping headers for other hosts; add a one-line deploy-preview verification (log resolved country / curl via non-DE VPN) to the E1 checklist.

accepted.title: No Open Graph / Twitter metadata anywhere despite the 'users can share' objective — og:url is an uncounted 7th URL construction site
accepted.claim: Deal generateMetadata emits only title/description/alternates; grep-verified zero openGraph/twitter fields in src/app. Shared deal links render without image or price on every platform, and OG tags are a primary extraction surface for answer/social scrapers. C2 rewrites this exact function anyway, and og:url falls under the spec's own single-source-of-truth rule.
accepted.evidence: src/app/[locale]/deal/[slug]/page.tsx:38-45 (verified: metadata object has no openGraph/twitter); grep 'openGraph|twitter' src/app → 0 hits (re-run during adjudication); spec line 94 enumerates exactly 6 sites.
accepted.fix: Todo C2 acceptance: add openGraph { title, description, url: absoluteDealUrl(...), images: [deal.imageUrl], locale } and twitter { card: 'summary_large_image' } to deal generateMetadata; site-default OG in the locale layout. Spec §4: count og:url as the 7th construction site routed through the helper.

accepted.title: JSON-LD block C3 already edits: no Product.url, semantically wrong AggregateOffer (offerCount:1, highPrice=strikethrough), no priceValidUntil, no BreadcrumbList
accepted.claim: Verified in page.tsx:59-92: the Product node has no top-level url (the canonical this spec exists to mint is never asserted on the entity); AggregateOffer with offerCount:1 misuses the multi-seller type and asserts the strikethrough original price as a live offer ceiling; priceValidUntil is absent (guaranteed RRT warning on every page, and expired pages will assert unbounded stale prices); no BreadcrumbList despite category hubs existing and C7 canonicalizing them. All four are cheap while C3 touches the block; note availability→OutOfStock itself is valid.
accepted.evidence: src/app/[locale]/deal/[slug]/page.tsx:59-92 (all four absences verified by read); grep priceValidUntil src → 0 hits; Google Product structured-data doc defines AggregateOffer for multi-seller aggregation and lists priceValidUntil as recommended.
accepted.fix: Todo C3 acceptance: add url: dealUrl on the Product node; replace AggregateOffer+nested Offer with a single Offer (price=salePrice, priceValidUntil derived from the expiry window, availability by status); add BreadcrumbList (Home › {category} › {product}) reusing C7's category URLs.

rejected.title: After Phase D the real rollback floor is the Phase-C build; 'redeploy previous app build' 404s live URLs if you ever roll back further (chaos)
rejected.reason: Overstated on re-verification. On a pre-C build after D's slug rewrite, DealCard links row.slug — which now holds the NEW-format slug — and getDealBySlug does an exact .eq('slug', segment) match (deals.repo.ts:139-153), so on-site navigation keeps working; only previously-published old-format URLs (C-window sitemap entries, shared links) and post-rename stale URLs 404. That is a bounded documentation gap, not 'no safe build older than C'. The legitimate residue (document the rollback floor; hold C in prod ≥1 week before D) is folded into the accepted blocker's plan changes. Note this was the ONLY finding of 49 that failed re-verification in a material way — every other confirmed claim I re-tested (regexes, birthday math, greps, schedules, layout cookies(), NFD deletions, index/query mismatch) checked out exactly as stated.

overallVerdict: SUSTAINABLE AFTER A SPEC REVISION — with three named decisions returned to the reviewer; the core design does NOT need to go back to the design panel. All six specialists independently converged on the same conclusion and none of them broke the identity core: frozen md5-derived public_id + trigger-owned slug composition + legacy_slug-forever + the drift-healing 308 ladder is the right architecture, and several attacked mechanics were positively verified sound (permanentRedirect-in-generateMetadata yields a real pre-shell 308, 308-vs-301 is a non-issue for Google/Bing, flat /deal/ paths match industry practice, price_history/transactions/alerts are all keyed on product_id so nothing downstream breaks). What fails is the spec TEXT, in three clusters. (1) One true blocker: the Phase C→D migration window bricks every deal URL (Step B self-308 loop plus hard 404s for the delta cohort) — fixed by a Step B equality guard + live-slug lookup and by moving the mint triggers/backfill into Phase B; this must land before B1 starts. (2) Sections written against capabilities the stack doesn't have: §6 sitemap should be rewritten wholesale rather than patched — four independent false premises (Next 14 generates no sitemap index; metadata routes freeze at build; PostgREST max-rows guts 10k shard queries; the 'documented Google pattern' for hreflang is the opposite of what Google documents, and 10k×14-alternate shards blow Netlify's response cap) — plus §4's revalidate rationale, which is dead config under the cookies()-bearing layout, and §5's expiry design, whose re-activation trigger has no discriminator and whose 48h window is justified against a 15-minute cadence that does not exist (deployed reality: daily cron, top-100-per-cell persistence). Every one of these has a bounded, local fix specified in the accepted findings. (3) Three items are POLICY choices that become irreversible the moment permanent URLs mint at Phase D, so they go back to the reviewer (not the panel) as Phase A decisions: country-in-identity vs one-row-per-product (kelkoo ids carry no country today — verified), slug_base freeze-after-mint vs recompute+308 (recommend freeze; recompute converts feed-title oscillation into browser-cached redirect loops), and fully-dynamic deal pages with a crawl budget vs a layout refactor to earn ISR. Recommended path: cut a v2 of the spec applying the 25 accepted fixes, re-sequence the plan (mint triggers in B; C hold period before D; delta backfill + gate in D1), and get the three reviewer decisions on paper — then Phase B is safe to start. Scorecard: 49 raw findings → 25 after dedup (1 blocker, 16 major, 8 minor), 1 rejected as overstated; notable downgrades: sitemap-hreflang blocker→major (on-page hreflang preserves variant discovery), expiry-trigger blocker→major (D3 staging gates would catch both failure modes before prod), middleware-geo major→minor (unverified header claim, pre-existing code).
